questions.rubric: Full credit (25 pts): (1) Correctly classifies A as concrete (has units 'ms' and a threshold '350') and B as vague (no units, no threshold, no test condition) with one-sentence justification each. (2) Rewrites B with explicit units and a threshold, e.g. 'Battery life must be >= 6 months at one reading per minute on a 1000 mAh Li-ion cell' or 'Deep-sleep current <= 20 usA'. Accept any specific, measurable form. (3) Explains how vague requirement causes bad partitioning: without knowing the target sleep current, the team cannot decide whether a ULP core wakeup circuit in hardware is necessary, or guesses wrong about which functions need hardware acceleration. Deduct 8 pts if rewrite lacks units or a numeric threshold. Deduct 8 pts if the partitioning-impact explanation is missing or only one sentence.

Full credit (25 pts): (1) Correct CPU% calculation: value_us / 200000 x 100. Expected answers by pool value -- 280 uss: 0.14%; 400 uss: 0.20%; 150 uss: 0.075%; 600 uss: 0.30%. Work must be shown. (2) Correctly concludes no hardware needed because the percentage is well below any meaningful threshold (accept any reasoning that references the low utilization number). (3) Identifies a crossover point when offloading becomes worth evaluating -- accept any answer in the range of 1-10% CPU headroom, e.g. 'if packets are sent every 3-5 ms, CPU overhead approaches 5-10%, making hardware worthwhile.' The exact number matters less than the reasoning. Deduct 10 pts if no calculation is shown. Flag for AI review if the student gives the correct percentage for a different pool value than their slot -- may indicate a guess rather than a calculation.

Full credit (25 pts): (1) Defines tape-out as sending the final RTL/layout to a foundry to generate photomasks for wafer fabrication; explains that any bug is etched into every die and fixing it requires new masks + new wafer run = months of delay and millions of dollars. (2) Recommends proceeding with the firmware workaround: 0.08% CPU overhead is negligible, the product ships on time, and no respin cost is incurred. A quantitative justification (0.08% << any meaningful budget) is required for full credit. (3) Names any valid firmware-appropriate function: complex communication protocol stack (TCP/IP, MQTT, Matter, TLS), OTA update logic, UI/UX behavior, business rules, configuration data, algorithm tuning parameters. The reasoning must reference the ability to patch/change post-ship. Deduct 8 pts if no quantitative justification for the proceed decision. Deduct 8 pts if the firmware-appropriate example is technically wrong or lacks a justification.

Full credit (25 pts): (1) Selects bare-metal register access for the ADC sampling routine with reasoning that 15 uss ESP-IDF overhead exceeds the 8 uss requirement and only direct register access can eliminate the HAL overhead. ESP-IDF or Arduino are wrong answers here -- both have > 8 uss overhead. (2) Names one valid ESP-IDF automation (FreeRTOS task scheduling, Wi-Fi/MQTT driver, NVS, OTA update, partition management, watchdog) and one valid trade-off (loss of register-level control, inability to configure undocumented peripheral bits, framework overhead in tight loops, portability vs. control). (3) Names any valid co-design pitfall relevant to mixing abstraction levels, e.g.: interface mismatch between bare-metal ISR and RTOS task sharing a buffer without proper synchronization; version skew if ESP-IDF is updated and bare-metal headers diverge; or insufficient debug hooks when the bare-metal routine silently corrupts data. Deduct 8 pts if the framework recommendation is incorrect (not bare-metal) without valid justification. Deduct 5 pts each for missing automation or trade-off.

This is an auto-graded hardware telemetry question. The firmware publishes: [hw:sw_us=X hw_us=Y speedup=Z blocks=1000]. Award full credit (10 pts) if ALL of the following conditions are met: (1) sw_us is in the plausible range 50-2000 (software AES-128 on ESP32 Xtensa takes ~100-500 uss per block at 240 MHz); (2) hw_us is in the range 1-50 (hardware AES engine is typically 2-6 uss); (3) hw_us < sw_us (hardware must be faster -- if not, hardware AES was not properly engaged); (4) speedup >= 5 (expect 50-200x in practice; anything below 5 suggests a firmware issue). Award 5 pts if the [hw:...] tag is present but one value is out of range (possible correct measurement on a non-standard board). Award 0 if the tag is absent or hw_us >= sw_us. Flag for instructor review if speedup < 10 or sw_us < 50 (may indicate the SW AES implementation was incorrectly using hardware).

Award full credit (10 pts) if: (1) student provides a packet rate threshold with a calculation -- correct approach is to find when sw_us / interval_us approaches some meaningful CPU% (accept any threshold between 1% and 20%, e.g. 'HW is worth it above 500 packets/sec because at that rate SW AES takes 500 x sw_us = X% CPU'); the exact threshold matters less than showing the ratio; (2) student names one valid production overhead NOT in the benchmark: key schedule cost (esp_aes_setkey each call), DMA buffer allocation, interrupt round-trip from HW engine to CPU, per-packet buffer copy cost, context switch overhead in RTOS tasks, or key management and rotation cost. Accept any technically sound overhead. Deduct 5 pts if no packet rate calculation is shown. Deduct 4 pts if the overhead named is already captured in the benchmark (e.g. 'the AES encrypt call itself' -- that IS what was measured).
